finding: Shared demographic knowledge about status distribution across geographical regions reverses the direction of a fundamental spatial-valence association in place preferences; an underlying metaphoric mapping (vertical-valence) remains stable across cultures, but its instantiation in cardinal direction choices flips when actual distribution of high-status populations reverses between locations.

Media representation of individuals with dual identities strategically emphasizes in-group identity during positive outcomes and out-group identity during negative outcomes, consistent with motivated self-enhancement processes operating on the identity dimension rather than the person's group membership.

Extended exposure to delayed reinforcement in early development produces large, lasting reductions in preference for smaller-sooner rewards compared to larger-later rewards; this effect persists after natural developmental improvements in self-control and exceeds the impact of extended exposure to immediate reinforcement.

Response force exerted to maintain visibility of preferred visual stimuli shows significant correspondence with independently measured stimulus preferences; conjugate schedules measuring force provide rapid assessment (minutes) of stimulus preference that correlates with ranked preference judgments.

Workplace intergenerational climate comprises five distinct but interrelated dimensions (contact, retention pressure, positive affect, inclusiveness, lack of stereotypes); these dimensions predict age-based stereotyping and mentoring behavior and are partially independent from general job satisfaction.

Personality traits predict both baseline body weight and weight changes over time, with gender-specific patterns: neuroticism predicts weight gain for both genders but more strongly for women; openness predicts weight loss for women only; conscientiousness protects against baseline weight but not changes over time.
